author: jacanidjar
body:
/claim #462

# feat: implement JSON Schema support (Draft 2020-12) #462

### Description
This Pull Request implements comprehensive **JSON Schema (Draft 2020-12)** support within the ZIO Schema ecosystem. It enables bidirectional conversion between ZIO Schemas and JSON Schemas, facilitating JSON data validation and automated documentation generation.

### Key Changes
- **New Data Type:** Added `JsonSchema` in `zio.blocks.schema.json` to represent the Draft 2020-12 structure.
- **Recursive Validation:** Implemented `JsonSchema.check` logic validating:
  - Primitive types and constant values/enums.
  - Objects (properties, required fields, additional properties).
  - Arrays (items, prefixItems, uniqueness, size constraints).
- **ZIO Integration:** Added `Schema.fromJsonSchema`, allowing the creation of a `Schema[Json]` that applies schema rules during decoding.
- **Automated Generation:** Updated `JsonBinaryCodecDeriver` to support `toJsonSchema`, enabling schema generation from any existing ZIO `Schema`.
- **Resilience:** Added a default `toJsonSchema` implementation in the `JsonBinaryCodec` base class to maintain compatibility and ease maintenance.

### Test Results
- `JsonSchemaSpec`: **7/7 Tests Passed** ✅ (Primitives, records, sequences, options, and validations like `minimum`).
- `JsonBinaryCodecDeriverSpec`: Verified compilation and functionality.

### Usage Example
```scala
// Generate JSON Schema from a ZIO Schema
val userJsonSchema = Schema[User].toJsonSchema

// Use a JSON Schema to validate decoding
val schema = Schema.fromJsonSchema(userJsonSchema)
val result = schema.decode(JsonFormat)(jsonBytes)

author: samuel-asleep
body:
This PR enhances the `json` string interpolator with context-aware compile-time validation for interpolations, improving type safety and error messages, and verifies behavior across supported Scala versions and platforms.

## Implementation status

**Key position**
- Stringable `PrimitiveType` values are accepted as object keys.
- Non-stringable types in key position fail at compile time with a clear, context-specific error.

**String literal position**
- Stringable `PrimitiveType` values can be interpolated inside JSON string literals with correct escaping.
- Non-stringable types inside string literals fail at compile time with a clear, context-specific error.

**Value position**
- Values are encoded using `JsonEncoder[A]` (including encoders derived from `Schema[A]`), consistent with current runtime behavior.
- Compile-time enforcement for value position is intentionally not applied (value encoding is resolved at runtime).

## Changes made
- Expanded the test suite to document and lock in compile-time checks for key and string-literal positions.
- Improved Scala.js compatibility in tests by using deterministic UUIDs (`UUID.fromString`) instead of `UUID.randomUUID`.

## Verification
- `JsonInterpolatorSpec` tests pass on Scala 2.13.18 and Scala 3.3.7.
- Schema tests pass on JVM, and the interpolator suite is verified on JVM and Scala.js.
- Verified on Scala Native as well.

Closes #801 

/claim #801
title: Enhance JSON interpolator with context-aware type checking

author: orbin123
body:
Add JSON Schema 2020-12 Support to ZIO Blocks

This PR adds comprehensive JSON Schema 2020-12 support to ZIO Blocks, enabling automatic schema generation, runtime validation, and round-trip serialization for any type with a Schema[A].

Overview

What: Full implementation of JSON Schema 2020-12 specification as a first-class ZIO Blocks feature
Why: Enables integration with external tools (OpenAPI, Swagger, validation services) and provides industry-standard JSON schema generation from Scala types
Scope: Core ADT, validation engine, schema derivation, and comprehensive test coverage

Key Features

  • ✅ Complete JSON Schema 2020-12 ADT - Full modeling of all vocabularies with 45+ keywords
  • ✅ Type-safe validation - Validate JSON against schemas with detailed error paths
  • ✅ Error accumulation - Collects all validation errors, not just the first
  • ✅ Schema composition - && (allOf), || (anyOf), ! (not) operators
  • ✅ Automatic derivation - Schema[A].toJsonSchema and JsonBinaryCodec[A].toJsonSchema
  • ✅ Format validation - 15+ built-in validators (date-time, email, uri, uuid, etc.)
  • ✅ $ref resolution - Internal reference support via SchemaRegistry

File Changes

Core Implementation (6 new files)

zio-blocks/schema/shared/src/main/scala/zio/blocks/schema/json/
├── JsonSchema.scala              [CREATED] - Core ADT with True/False/SchemaObject
├── JsonSchemaCodec.scala         [CREATED] - Bidirectional JSON serialization
├── JsonSchemaError.scala         [CREATED] - Structured validation errors
├── JsonSchemaValidator.scala     [CREATED] - Full 2020-12 validation engine
├── FormatValidators.scala        [CREATED] - String format validators
└── SchemaRegistry.scala          [CREATED] - $ref resolution registry

Integration (3 modified files)

├── Schema.scala                  [MODIFIED] - Added toJsonSchema method
├── JsonBinaryCodec.scala         [MODIFIED] - Added toJsonSchema to trait
└── JsonBinaryCodecDeriver.scala  [MODIFIED] - Schema generation for derived types

Tests (6 new test suites, ~135 tests)

schema/shared/src/test/scala/zio/blocks/schema/json/
├── JsonSchemaSpec.scala                  - Core validation (~45 tests)
├── SchemaToJsonSchemaSpec.scala          - Schema[A] conversion (~20 tests)
├── JsonBinaryCodecToJsonSchemaSpec.scala - Codec extraction (~25 tests)
├── SchemaFromJsonSchemaSpec.scala        - Validation integration (~20 tests)
├── JsonSchemaRoundTripSpec.scala         - Serialization (~15 tests)
└── UnevaluatedSpec.scala                 - unevaluated* keywords (~10 tests)

Example Usage

Generate schema from case class:

case class Person(name: String, age: Int, email: Option[String])
implicit val schema: Schema[Person] = Schema.derived

val jsonSchema = Schema[Person].toJsonSchema
// {"type": "object", "properties": {...}, "required": ["name", "age"]}

Validate JSON at runtime:

val schema = JsonSchema.`object`(
  "name" -> JsonSchema.string.withMinLength(1),
  "age"  -> JsonSchema.integer.withMinimum(0)
).withRequired("name", "age")

schema.validate(validJson)   // Right(())
schema.validate(invalidJson) // Left(JsonSchemaError(...))

Create validated Schema[Json]:

val requestSchema = JsonSchema.`object`(
  "action" -> JsonSchema.`enum`(Json.str("create"), Json.str("update"))
).withRequired("action")

val schema: Schema[Json] = Schema.fromJsonSchema(requestSchema)

Validation Mapping

ZIO Blocks Type | JSON Schema Output -- | -- Schema[String] | {"type": "string"} Schema[Int] | {"type": "integer"} Schema[Instant] | {"type": "string", "format": "date-time"} Schema[List[A]] | {"type": "array", "items": {...}} Validation.Numeric.Positive | {"exclusiveMinimum": 0} Validation.String.Pattern(regex) | {"pattern": "..."}

Testing

All tests pass on JVM:

sbt schemaJVM/test
sbt "schemaJVM/testOnly *JsonSchema*"

Coverage: 135+ tests across 6 suites covering all keywords, validation scenarios, composition, and error accumulation.

Checklist

  • ✅ All JVM tests pass
  • ✅ Code formatted with scalafmt
  • ✅ No compiler warnings
  • ✅ Public APIs documented
  • ✅ No breaking changes
  • ✅ Follows project conventions

Implementation Notes

  • Full 2020-12 spec: All vocabularies implemented for completeness
  • Error accumulation: Collects all failures for better UX
  • EvaluationContext: Tracks evaluated properties/items per 2020-12 spec
  • Known limitation: Recursive types may overflow on Scala Native (works on JVM/JS)
/claim #462 fixes #462
title: Add JSON Schema 2020-12 Support to ZIO Blocks Schema

author: jdegoes
body:
## Summary

This PR improves the `JsonSelection` API by fixing naming inconsistencies and improving orthogonality of operations.

## Changes

### Commit 1: Rename filtering methods and remove extraction methods

**Filtering method renames (for consistency with `objects`/`arrays`):**
- `stringValues` → `strings`
- `numberValues` → `numbers`
- `booleanValues` → `booleans`
- `nullValues` → `nulls`

**Removed extraction methods:**
- `strings`, `string`, `numbers`, `number`, `booleans`, `boolean`, `int`, `long`, `float`, `double`

These were redundant since `.as[T]` provides the same functionality with more flexibility (works with any type that has a `JsonDecoder`).

### Commit 2: Improve API orthogonality and naming

**Field rename:**
- `value` → `either` (follows ZIO conventions)

**Removed redundant methods:**
- `toEither` (use `.either` field directly)
- `all` as alias (was duplicate of `toEither`)
- `headOption` (use `.any.toOption` instead)

**Condenser method renames for clarity:**
| Old | New | Semantics |
|-----|-----|-----------|
| `single` | `one` | Exactly 1 value required |
| `first` | `any` | At least 1, returns first |
| `one` | `all` | Condenses all values (wraps multiple in array) |

**Pluralized strict type filters (since selections contain multiple values):**
- `asObject` → `asObjects`
- `asArray` → `asArrays`
- `asString` → `asStrings`
- `asNumber` → `asNumbers`
- `asBoolean` → `asBooleans`
- `asNull` → `asNulls`

## Rationale

1. **Naming clarity**: `one` semantically means "give me the one value", `any` means "give me any value", `all` means "give me all values condensed"
2. **Reduced redundancy**: Removed methods that duplicated functionality available through other means
3. **Consistency**: All type filtering methods now use plural naming consistently
4. **ZIO conventions**: Field renamed to `either` to match ZIO patterns

## Testing

- All tests pass on Scala 2.13.18 and Scala 3.3.7
- Updated documentation in `docs/reference/json.md`
title: Improve JsonSelection API consistency and orthogonality

author: jdegoes
body:
## Summary

Refactors `DerivedOptics` to allow parameterless usage and fixes singleton type preservation for case objects and enum singletons.

## Key Changes

### Scala 2
- `trait DerivedOptics` is now parameterless
- `optics` method uses `DerivedOpticsMacros.opticsFromCompanionImpl` to infer type from companion object
- `DerivedOptics.Of[S]` available for cases where type cannot be inferred (generic instantiations, type aliases)

### Scala 3
- Uses Kit Langton's companion-object conversion trick with `CompanionClass` + `OpticsFor` type classes
- Enables direct field access (e.g., `Person.name` instead of `Person.optics.name`)
- User-defined members on companion objects take precedence over derived optics

### Bug Fix: Singleton Type Preservation
Fixed an issue where prisms for case objects and enum singletons lost their singleton type:
- **Before**: `Status.active` was typed as `Prism[Status, Status]`
- **After**: `Status.active` is correctly typed as `Prism[Status, Status.Active.type]`

Root causes fixed:
- **Scala 3**: Changed `child.termRef.widen` to `child.termRef` to preserve singleton type
- **Scala 2**: Added check for `isModuleClass` and use `module.typeSignature` instead of `toType`

## Testing

- All tests pass on Scala 2.13.18 and 3.3.7
- Added tests for direct field access in Scala 3
- Added tests for `DerivedOptics.Of[S]` pattern
- Added explicit type annotation tests for singleton prisms
title: refactor(DerivedOptics): eliminate type parameter S from trait

author: Copilot
body:
Addresses feedback that `Name` (a `zio.prelude.Newtype[String]`) wasn't getting primitive/newtype optimization due to ending the chain with `.withTypeName`.

## Changes

- Changed `Name` schema from `.withTypeName[Name]` to `.asOpaqueType[Name]`

## Context

`withTypeName` only sets the type name. `asOpaqueType` sets both the type name and `wrapperPrimitiveType` for optimized register storage—the intended behavior for opaque types where runtime representation matches the underlying primitive.

```scala
// Before
implicit val schema: Schema[Name] = Schema[String]
  .transformOrFail[Name](...)
  .withTypeName[Name]  // Missing optimization

// After  
implicit val schema: Schema[Name] = Schema[String]
  .transformOrFail[Name](...)
  .asOpaqueType[Name]  // Enables primitive type optimization
```

<!-- START COPILOT CODING AGENT TIPS -->
---

💡 You can make Copilot smarter by setting up custom instructions, customizing its development environment and configuring Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers. Learn more [Copilot coding agent tips](https://gh.io/copilot-coding-agent-tips) in the docs.
title: Use asOpaqueType for Newtype schemas to enable primitive type optimization

author: Copilot
body:
The `Name` schema in `ZIOPreludeSupportSpec.scala` was using `.withTypeName[Name]`, which only updates the TypeName but leaves `wrapperPrimitiveType = None`, preventing optimized register storage.

## Changes

- Changed `.withTypeName[Name]` to `.asOpaqueType[Name]` in the `Name` schema definition

Since `Name` is a `zio.prelude.Newtype[String]` with identical runtime representation to its underlying primitive, `.asOpaqueType` correctly sets both the TypeName and `wrapperPrimitiveType` for optimized storage.

```scala
object Name extends Newtype[String] {
  override inline def assertion: zio.prelude.Assertion[String] = !zio.prelude.Assertion.isEmptyString

  implicit val schema: Schema[Name] = Schema[String]
    .transformOrFail(
      s =>
        if (s.length > 0) Right(s.asInstanceOf[Name])
        else Left(SchemaError.validationFailed("String must not be empty")),
      _.asInstanceOf[String]
    )
    .asOpaqueType[Name]  // Changed from .withTypeName[Name]
}
```

This aligns with the pattern used in `NeotypeSupportSpec.scala` for neotype schemas.

<!-- START COPILOT CODING AGENT TIPS -->
---

author: Copilot
body:
Addresses feedback from #818 regarding `PosInt` schema definitions. Since `PosInt` is an `AnyVal` wrapper with runtime representation as `Int`, it should use `.asOpaqueType[PosInt]` to enable the primitive optimization path, not `.withTypeName[PosInt]`.

## Changes

- Updated `PosInt` schema definitions in `JsonBinaryCodecDeriverSpec`, `SchemaSpec`, and `DynamicOpticSpec` to use `.asOpaqueType[PosInt]`

```scala
// Before
implicit val schema: Schema[PosInt] =
  Schema[Int].transformOrFail[PosInt](PosInt.apply, _.value).withTypeName[PosInt]

// After
implicit val schema: Schema[PosInt] =
  Schema[Int].transformOrFail[PosInt](PosInt.apply, _.value).asOpaqueType[PosInt]
```

`.withTypeName` is appropriate for regular case class wrappers (like `UserId`, `Email`), while `.asOpaqueType` sets `wrapperPrimitiveType` for value classes to enable primitive encoding.

author: jdegoes
body:
This PR introduces a cleaner API for creating wrapper/newtype schemas:

## New Methods

- `Schema#transform[B](to: A => B, from: B => A): Schema[B]` - for total transformations
- `Schema#transformOrFail[B](to: A => Either[SchemaError, B], from: B => A): Schema[B]` - for partial transformations with validation
- `Schema#withTypeName[B](implicit typeName: TypeName[B]): Schema[B]` - sets TypeName for case class wrappers
- `Schema#asOpaqueType[B](implicit typeName: TypeName[B]): Schema[B]` - sets TypeName AND primitive type for opaque types/newtypes
- `TypeName.derived[A]` - implicit macro to derive TypeName for any type

## Why This Change

The existing `Schema#wrap` and `Schema#wrapTotal` methods have a confusing API:

```scala
// Old API - confusing direction
Schema.derived[Email].wrap[String](wrap, unwrap)
```

The method is called on Schema[A], requires Schema[B], but returns Schema[A]. The direction of transformation is backwards from what you'd expect.

## New API

```scala
// For case class wrappers
Schema[String]
  .transformOrFail(Email.make, _.value)
  .withTypeName[Email]

// For opaque types / newtypes (same runtime representation as primitive)
Schema[Int]
  .transform(Age.apply, Age.unwrap)
  .asOpaqueType[Age]
```

Read left-to-right: start with source schema, transform to target, set typename.

## Key Difference: withTypeName vs asOpaqueType

- `withTypeName`: For case class wrappers where the runtime type differs from the primitive
- `asOpaqueType`: For opaque types/newtypes where the runtime representation IS the primitive (enables optimized register storage)

## Migration

The old methods are deprecated. Migration:

| Old | New |
|-----|-----|
| `Schema.derived[A].wrap[B](f, g)` (case class) | `Schema[B].transformOrFail(f, g).withTypeName[A]` | | `Schema.derived[A].wrap[B](f, g)` (opaque type) | `Schema[B].transformOrFail(f, g).asOpaqueType[A]` | | `Schema.derived[A].wrapTotal[B](f, g)` (case class) | `Schema[B].transform(f, g).withTypeName[A]` | | `Schema.derived[A].wrapTotal[B](f, g)` (opaque type) | `Schema[B].transform(f, g).asOpaqueType[A]` |
title: Add Schema#transform, #transformOrFail, #withTypeName, and #asOpaqueType

author: Pranjal6955
body:
### Description : 
This PR implements a robust, type-safe json string interpolator macro for ZIO Schema. It enables compile-time validation of JSON syntax and strict type checking for interpolated variables, preventing runtime errors and ensuring type safety.

### Key Features :

- **Context-Aware Interpolation:** The macro intelligently detects whether an interpolation hole is a JSON key, a value, or part of a string literal.
       - **Keys:** Must be of a "Stringable" primitive type (e.g., Int, UUID, LocalDate). Non-string keys are automatically converted to strings and quoted.
       - **Values:** Must have an implicit JsonEncoder available.
       - **String Literals:** Variables inside JSON strings are properly escaped and inserted.

- **Raw String Support:** Interpolated strings are handled efficiently without intermediate JSON object creation, using a new Raw wrapper in the runtime.

- **Compile-Time Validation:**
      - Validates JSON syntax at compile-time.
      - Reports clear error messages for invalid types or syntax errors.

- **Cross-Compilation:** Support for both Scala 2 and Scala 3 macros.

### Changes:

**Macros:**

- `schema/js-jvm/src/main/scala-2/zio/blocks/schema/json/package.scala` : Implemented Scala 2 macro with blackbox.Context parser.
- `schema/js-jvm/src/main/scala-3/zio/blocks/schema/json/package.scala` : Implemented Scala 3 macro with Quotes and Expr parser.

**Runtime:**

- `schema/shared/src/main/scala/zio/blocks/schema/json/JsonInterpolatorRuntime.scala` : Added Raw case class and writeEscapedString for optimize string writing.

**Tests:**

- `schema/shared/src/test/scala/zio/blocks/schema/json/JsonInterpolatorSpec.scala` : Added 25+ tests covering all interpolation contexts, primitive types, complex types, and failure cases.

Fixes: #801 
/claim #801
title: feat: Implemented Type-Safe JSON Interpolator

author: samuel-asleep
body:
This change enhances the `json` string interpolator with stricter, more predictable type-safety and clearer compile-time diagnostics, while preserving all existing behavior.

### What changed
The interpolator now enforces context-aware rules at compile time:

**Key position**
- Only stringable primitive types are allowed when interpolating JSON object keys.
- Invalid key interpolations fail at compile time with clear, actionable error messages.

**Value position**
- Any type with an available `JsonEncoder[A]` is accepted in value position.
- Missing encoders are reported at compile time with diagnostics that explain what is required and how to provide it (including deriving from `Schema[A]`).

**String literal interpolation**
- Interpolation inside JSON string values is supported.
- Only stringable primitive types are allowed inside string literals.
- Invalid cases fail at compile time with context-specific error messages.

**Diagnostics**
- All failures clearly report:
  - the interpolation context (key / value / string literal),
  - the provided type,
  - the required constraint.

### Tests
- Expanded test coverage to validate:
  - all stringable primitive types in key position,
  - all stringable primitive types inside string literals,
  - value interpolation for types with `JsonEncoder[A]` (including derived encoders),
  - compile-time failures for unsupported cases.
- Existing tests remain unchanged and continue to pass across supported platforms.

### Incidental fixes
- Comma flag in `Map` / `Iterable` serialization moved outside the `foreach` scope.
- Escape sequence detection now counts consecutive backslashes correctly.

### Issue
Resolves and completes the requirements discussed in:
https://github.com/zio/zio-blocks/issues/801

/claim #801

Related: #801
title: Improve JSON interpolator type-safety and diagnostics across keys, va…

author: bikramadhikari001
body:
/claim #801

## Add String Literal Interpolation Support to JSON Interpolator

Fixes #801

### Summary

This PR adds support for interpolating values inside JSON string literals, allowing expressions like:

```scala
val userId = UUID.randomUUID()
json"""{"id": "user-$userId"}"""
```

### Implementation

**Runtime Support:**
- Added `jsonWithInterpolationAndContext()` method that accepts metadata about which interpolations are inside strings
- Added `writeStringLiteralValue()` method that converts values to strings and escapes JSON special characters
- Proper JSON character escaping for: `"`, `\`, `\n`, `\t`, `\r`, `\f`, `\b`, and Unicode control characters

**Compile-Time Support:**
- Added context detection in all 4 macro implementations (Scala 2/3 × JVM-JS/Native)
- Added compile-time type checking for Scala 2 that validates `PrimitiveType[A]` instances for string literal interpolations
- Fixed cumulative state tracking for multiple interpolations in the same string
- Note: Compile-time type checking is disabled for Scala 3 due to limitations with implicit search in macros. Runtime type checking still catches all type errors.

**Tests:**
- 12 basic test suites covering all PrimitiveType types in string literals
- 4 property-based tests using generators (Int, UUID, LocalDate, Instant)
- 3 compile-time error tests using `typeCheck`
- All 85 JsonInterpolatorSpec tests pass on JVM and JS
- All 1957 schema tests pass on JVM

### Files Changed

- `schema/shared/src/main/scala/zio/blocks/schema/json/JsonInterpolatorRuntime.scala` - Runtime support
- `schema/js-jvm/src/main/scala-2/zio/blocks/schema/json/package.scala` - Scala 2 JVM/JS macro with type checking
- `schema/js-jvm/src/main/scala-3/zio/blocks/schema/json/package.scala` - Scala 3 JVM/JS macro
- `schema/native/src/main/scala-2/zio/blocks/schema/json/package.scala` - Scala 2 Native macro
- `schema/native/src/main/scala-3/zio/blocks/schema/json/package.scala` - Scala 3 Native macro
- `schema/shared/src/test/scala/zio/blocks/schema/json/JsonInterpolatorSpec.scala` - Comprehensive tests

### Testing

✅ All 1957 tests pass on JVM (Scala 2.13 & 3.3)
✅ All 85 JsonInterpolatorSpec tests pass on JS (Scala 2.13 & 3.3)
✅ Native compiles successfully (Scala 3.3)
✅ All formatting checks pass (`scalafmtCheckAll`, `scalafmtSbtCheck`)
✅ All lint checks pass for Scala 2.13, 3.3, and 3.7
title: Add string literal interpolation support to JSON interpolator

author: Nanashi-lab
body:
### Joint and Split for nested fields. 

` _.address.street + _.address.city → _.address.fullAddress` -> this is done

`_.address.street + _.origin.country → _.address.fullAddress` -> This gets complex since now we have to extract values from multiple nested source paths, combine to targeted nested path, remove the nested source fields, and rebuilthe whole structure immutability.

So if the parent is not the same, it gracefully errors out. 

### No Serialization
DynamicMigration is Pure ADT and can be inspected structurally, but it cannot be fully serialize/deserialize it due to existential types in SchemaExpr. `SchemaExpr[DynamicValue, ?]`, not sure how to deal with this.

### Reverse
Best effort has been made to reverse.  

Migration Action | Reverse Operation | Reversibility Status & Notes
-- | -- | --
Rename | Rename (flipped) | Lossless ✅  Perfect reversion.
RenameCase | RenameCase (flipped) | Lossless ✅  Perfect reversion.
AddField | DropField | Lossless ✅  Can cleanly remove the added field.
DropField | AddField (default) | Lossy ⚠️  Original value is lost; the reverse uses a default value.
Optionalize | Mandate | Lossless ✅  Can unwrap Some perfectly.
Mandate (Some) | Optionalize | Lossless ✅  Can wrap back to Optional.
Mandate (None) | Optionalize | Lossy ⚠️  Ambiguous; don't know if the original was None or Some(default).
TransformValue (Add/Sub/Mul/Div/Not) | Inverse Math | Lossless ✅  Perfect arithmetic inverse.
TransformValue (String case) | Opposite Case | Lossy ⚠️  Loses original casing information.
TransformValue (others) | Same Transform | Not Possible 🚫  Returns identity (no effective reverse).
ChangeType | Reverse Converter | Lossy ⚠️  Potential overflow or precision loss.
Join (StringConcat pattern) | Split | Lossy ⚠️  Works only if data perfectly matches the pattern.
Join (other) | Split (combiner as-is) | Lossy / Fails 🚫  May fail at runtime.
Split (StringSplit, 2 targets) | Join | Lossy ⚠️  Works only for 2-part splits.
Split (other) | Join (identity) | Lossy 🚫  Returns field0 only; other data lost.
TransformElements (Keys/Values) | Inverse Transform | Depends entirely on the specific transform logic.
TransformCase | Reversed Actions | Depends entirely on the nested actions involved.

/claim #519
/closes #519
title: Schema Migration for zio-blocks

author: bikramadhikari001
body:
## Summary

**Resolves #519** - Schema Migration System for ZIO Schema 2

/claim #519

This PR implements a comprehensive schema migration system for ZIO Schema 2, addressing issue #519. The implementation provides pure, serializable migrations that enable safe, reversible transformations of data structures as schemas evolve over time.

## Key Features

- **Pure Data Migrations**: Migrations are represented as data structures rather than functions, enabling serialization, introspection, and automatic reversal
- **Type-Safe API**: Compile-time checking with schemas ensures correctness
- **Macro Validation**: The `build()` method validates at compile-time that migrations are complete, catching missing field mappings before runtime
- **Deep Nesting Support**: Navigate and transform arbitrarily nested structures up to 7+ levels deep
- **Comprehensive Action Set**: 14 migration action types covering records, variants, and collections
- **Automatic Reversal**: Every migration has a structural inverse that can be generated automatically
- **Composable**: Combine migrations using the `++` operator for complex transformations
- **Rich Error Reporting**: Detailed path-based error messages for debugging

## Implementation Highlights

### Spec Compliance
- ✅ **Selector-based API** (Spec line 504): User API uses selector functions (`S => A`) for type-safe field access with IDE support
- ✅ **Compile-time validation**: Macro-based validation ensures complete migrations (adapted from PR #659 approach)
- ✅ **Structural type support**: Handles case classes, sealed traits, tuples, collections, and recursive types
- ✅ **Bidirectional coercion**: Automatic type conversions (e.g., Int ↔ Long, Tuple ↔ Case Class)
- ✅ **Pure data migrations**: All migrations are serializable data structures, not functions

### Cross-Platform Support
- **Scala 2.13 & 3.x**: Full cross-compilation with version-specific macro implementations
- **JVM, JS, Native**: All tests pass on all platforms
- **Type-safe macros**: Scala 2 uses whitebox macros, Scala 3 uses inline macros with quotes

## Implementation Details

### Core Architecture

The migration system is built on three layers:

1. **Migration[A, B]**: Type-safe wrapper with source and target schemas
2. **DynamicMigration**: Untyped core that operates on DynamicValue
3. **MigrationAction**: Individual transformation actions (14 types)

### Migration Actions

**Record Actions:**
- AddField: Add new fields with default values
- DropField: Remove existing fields
- Rename: Rename fields
- Optionalize: Make mandatory fields optional
- Mandate: Make optional fields mandatory
- ChangeType: Change field types with transformations
- TransformValue: Transform field values
- Join: Combine multiple fields into one
- Split: Split one field into multiple

**Variant Actions:**
- RenameCase: Rename variant cases
- TransformCase: Transform case values

**Collection Actions:**
- TransformElements: Transform sequence elements
- TransformKeys: Transform map keys
- TransformValues: Transform map values

### Builder API

The MigrationBuilder provides two complementary APIs for constructing migrations:

#### 1. Selector-Based API (Recommended)

Type-safe, IDE-friendly API using selector functions (`S => A`) as specified in the requirements:

```scala
val migration = MigrationBuilder(sourceSchema, targetSchema)
  .renameField(_.name, _.fullName)           // Type-safe field selection
  .addField(_.country, "USA")                // Compile-time field validation
  .dropField(_.oldField)                     // IDE autocomplete support
  .build
```

**Benefits:**
- **Type safety**: Compile errors if fields don't exist
- **IDE support**: Autocomplete and refactoring work seamlessly
- **Macro-powered**: Field names extracted at compile-time from lambda expressions
- **Cross-platform**: Works on both Scala 2.13 and Scala 3.x

#### 2. String-Based API (Alternative)

Traditional string-based API for dynamic scenarios:

```scala
val migration = MigrationBuilder(sourceSchema, targetSchema)
  .renameFieldByName("name", "fullName")
  .addFieldWithDefault("email", "unknown@example.com")
  .build
```

#### Compile-Time Validation

The `build()` method includes **compile-time macro validation** that ensures migrations are complete:
- All source fields are handled (renamed, dropped, or implicitly carried over)
- All target fields are produced (added, renamed, or implicitly carried over)
- Clear compile-time error messages for incomplete migrations

A `buildUnchecked()` method is also available for cases where validation is not needed.

## Testing

Comprehensive test suite with 66 tests covering:

- All 14 migration action types
- **Selector-based API**: 4 tests for type-safe field selection with different default value types
- Flat structure migrations
- Nested migrations (2-3 levels)
- Deep nested migrations (4-7 levels)
- Round-trip migrations (reversibility)
- Error cases with detailed error messages
- Composition and chaining
- Property-based tests (reversibility, composition, identity, idempotence)

All tests pass successfully on Scala 2.13 (1887 tests) and Scala 3.3 (1952 tests) across JVM, JS, and Native platforms.

## Documentation

Complete documentation following the zio-blocks style:

- Overview and motivation
- Core concepts and architecture
- Complete API reference for all action types
- Real-world examples (user evolution, API responses, database migrations, event sourcing, configuration)
- Advanced patterns (bidirectional migrations, conditional migrations, migration chains, versioned data storage)
- Testing strategies (unit tests, property-based tests, integration tests)
- Best practices and guidelines
- Performance considerations
- Common pitfalls and solutions
- Comparison with other approaches

## Example Usage

### Selector-Based API (Recommended)

```scala
// Define schemas
case class PersonV1(name: String, age: Int)
case class PersonV2(fullName: String, age: Int, country: String)

// Create migration with type-safe selectors
val migration = MigrationBuilder(
  Schema.derived[PersonV1],
  Schema.derived[PersonV2]
)
  .renameField(_.name, _.fullName)  // Type-safe field selection
  .addField(_.country, "USA")       // IDE autocomplete works!
  .build                            // Compile-time validation

// Apply migration
val v1 = PersonV1("Alice", 30)
val v2: PersonV2 = migration.migrate(v1).toOption.get
// PersonV2("Alice", 30, "USA")
```

### String-Based API (Alternative)

```scala
// Same migration using string-based API
val migration = MigrationBuilder(
  Schema.derived[PersonV1],
  Schema.derived[PersonV2]
)
  .renameFieldByName("name", "fullName")
  .addFieldWithDefault("country", "USA")
  .build

// Apply migration
val person = PersonV1("Alice", 30)
val result = migration.apply(person)
// Right(PersonV2("Alice", 30, "USA"))

// Reverse migration
val reversed = migration.reverse.apply(result.toOption.get)
// Right(PersonV1("Alice", 30))
```

## Addresses Issue

Closes #519
title: Add Schema Migration System for ZIO Schema 2

author: natinew77-creator
body:
# MessagePack Support for ZIO Schema 2

/claim #682

I have implemented full MessagePack support in the new `schema-messagepack` module, porting and improving upon the legacy functionality for ZIO Schema 2.

### Implementation Overview

The core logic resides in `MessagePackFormat.scala`, providing a `Deriver` that generates `MessagePackBinaryCodec` instances. This implementation is not a direct translation but an upgrade designed for production stability.

**Key Technical Decisions:**

1.  **Forward Compatibility via Map Encoding**:
    I implemented Record encoding using MessagePack Maps (key-value pairs) rather than Arrays. While slightly more verbose, this guarantees forward compatibility. Decoders explicitly skip unknown keys, allowing producers to add fields without breaking existing consumers—a critical requirement for distributed systems.

2.  **DoS Protection Integration**:
    Security was prioritized by enforcing bounds checks during decoding. The implementation rejects collections larger than 10 million elements and binary/string payloads exceeding 100MB, preventing Out-Of-Memory vectors.

3.  **Recursive Type Safety**:
    Complex recursive schemas (e.g., self-referencing Records) are handled using a `ThreadLocal` cache during derivation to prevent stack overflow errors.

4.  **Validation Support**:
    I restored strict validation for types like `Email` using `Schema.wrap`. This ensures that domain rules defined in the Schema are respected during deserialization.

### Verification

The `schema-messagepack` module passes **90/90 tests**, exceeding the coverage of the original version (~77 tests). The suite covers:
- All 30+ primitive types (including Time, UUID, Currency).
- Complex collections (Maps with String/Non-String keys, Sets, Arrays).
- Forward compatibility scenarios (extra fields in input).
- Edge cases (Negative BigInts, empty collections, deep nesting).

### Included Files
- `schema-messagepack/src/main/scala/zio/blocks/schema/messagepack/MessagePackFormat.scala`: Core derivation logic.
- `schema-messagepack/src/main/scala/zio/blocks/schema/messagepack/MessagePackBinaryCodec.scala`: Base codec abstraction.
- `schema-messagepack/src/test/scala/zio/blocks/schema/messagepack/MessagePackFormatSpec.scala`: Comprehensive test suite.
- `schema-messagepack/README.md`: Module documentation.

The code reflects a focus on correctness, security, and maintainability.




## Demo Video



https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/561b7a01-0ede-4cac-972f-7b06c6e16da8
title: feat: Port MessagePack support to ZIO Schema 2

author: natinew77-creator
body:
This PR introduces full BSON support for ZIO Schema 2 via the new `schema-bson` module, enabling seamless MongoDB integration for ZIO applications.

### **Overview**
This implementation provides a robust, type-safe way to serialize ZIO Schema types to and from BSON. It is a direct port and modernization of the logic from `zio-schema-bson`, adapted to leverage the new `Reflect` API and architecture of ZIO Schema 2.

### **Key Features**
*   **Complete Schema Support**: Handles all ZIO Schema primitives, including **Records** (case classes), **Enums** (sealed traits), **Collections** (List, Set, Map), and **Primitives** (Int, String, instant, UUID, etc.).
*   **Configurable Sum Types**: Supports multiple strategies for encoding sealed traits:
    *   **Wrapper Object** (Default): `{"ClassName": { ... }}`
    *   **Discriminator Field**: `{"type": "ClassName", ... }` via `@Modifier.config`
    *   **Flat/No Discriminator**: `{"field": "value"}` for simplified structures.
*   **Advanced Modifiers**: Full support for field-level customization using standard schema annotations:
    *   `@Modifier.rename("newName")`
    *   `@Modifier.alias("legacyName")`
    *   `@Modifier.transient` (for skipping fields/cases)
*   **Optimized Enum Encoding**: Enums are efficiently encoded as `BsonString` values when possible.
*   **Value Class Support**: Optimization for `AnyVal` types (like `CustomerId(id: ObjectId)`) to serialize as their underlying raw BSON type, ensuring efficiency.

### **Implementation Details**
*   **Module**: `schema-bson`
*   **Core Logic**: `BsonSchemaCodec` recursively traverses the `Reflect` ADT to build `BsonEncoder` and `BsonDecoder` instances.
*   **Verification**:
    *   Ported the complete test suite from the legacy module.
    *   **218/218 tests passing** (`BsonSchemaCodecSpec`).
    *   Includes round-trip tests for complex nested structures, recursive types, and all configuration permutations.

## Demo Video

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/3ae026d6-d937-4322-b3a6-1a33330bea56



### **Fixes**
Resolves #683 by delivering main-line BSON support.

/claim #683
title: feat: Port BSON support to ZIO Schema 2 (Fixes #683)

author: KrishnaShuk
body:
### Summary
This PR introduces a native Json AST to zio-blocks, addressing the need for unstructured data manipulation while maintaining strict integration with the library's core schema architecture.

### Architectural Design

- **Schema Integration:** The Json type is designed as a peer to DynamicValue. It supports lossless bidirectional conversion, allowing Json to participate fully in schema derivation and transformation pipelines.
- **Zero-Overhead Parsing:**  Rather than introducing a new parsing stack, the implementation wraps the existing JsonBinaryCodec. This ensures that Json parsing shares the same performance characteristics and configuration (e.g., JsonReader optimizations) as generated codecs.
- **Type Safety:**  The API exposes as[A] and from[A] methods, leveraging implicitly available JsonDecoder instances to bridge the gap between the AST and domain models safely.

### Implementation Details

- **Interpolators:**  Implemented json"" macros for both Scala 2 (whitebox/blackbox context) and Scala 3 (Quotes/Splices) to provide compile-time safety where possible and convenient syntax for complex object construction.
- **Navigation:**  Introduced DynamicOptic-based navigation, allowing for precise, immutable modification of deeply nested structures without boilerplate.
- **Cross-Compilation:**  Full support for Scala 2.13 and Scala 3.3, handling macro divergence explicitly.

### Verification

- **Property-Based Testing:**  Comprehensive suite (JsonSpec) verifying round-trip laws between Json <-> String and Json <-> DynamicValue.
- **Coverage:** 9/9 core property tests and 4/4 interpolator tests passing on all target platforms.

### Documentation

Added docs/reference/json.md detailing the ADT structure, optics usage, and interpolation examples.
### Fixes
Closes #679
/claim https://github.com/zio/zio-blocks/issues/679

author: natinew77-creator
body:
/claim #679

## Summary

Complete implementation of the `Json` data type for Issue #679. Introduces first-class JSON representation to zio-blocks with type-safe manipulation and deep Schema integration.

---

## What This Adds

### Core Json ADT
- Six standard types: `Object`, `Array`, `String`, `Number`, `Boolean`, `Null`
- Exhaustive pattern matching support
- `Number` stores values as strings to preserve full precision
- Convenience constructors for all Scala numeric types

### Navigation and Selection
- `JsonSelection` for fluent traversal with error accumulation
- Path expressions via `DynamicOptic`, array indices, or object keys
- Terminal operations: `one`, `first`, `toArray`

### Modification Operations
| Method | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `set` / `setOrFail` | Replace value at path |
| `delete` / `deleteOrFail` | Remove value at path |
| `insert` / `insertOrFail` | Insert into arrays |
| `modify` / `modifyOrFail` | Transform value at path |

### Transformation and Folding
- `transformUp` / `transformDown` for bottom-up and top-down traversal
- `transformKeys` for bulk field renaming
- `foldUp` / `foldDown` with `OrFail` variants
- `filter` / `filterNot` on path-value pairs

### Merging
Five built-in strategies:
- **Auto** / **Deep** - Recursive object merge with array concatenation
- **Shallow** - Top-level replacement only
- **Concat** - Array-focused combination
- **Replace** - Simple overwrite
- **Custom** - User-defined merge function

### Normalization and Diffing
- `normalize` - Sort keys, standardize numbers
- `sortKeys` - Recursive key ordering
- `dropNulls` / `dropEmpty` - Remove null values and empty containers
- `diff` - Produce RFC 6902 `JsonPatch` between two values

### Patching
- `patch` / `patchUnsafe` - Apply `JsonPatch` operations
- Supports: `add`, `remove`, `replace`, `move`, `copy`, `test`
- `Batch` wrapper for composing multiple operations

### Validation
- `check` / `conforms` for basic `JsonSchema` validation
- Covers primitives, arrays, objects with required fields, optionals, unions

### Encoding and Decoding
**Input formats:** `String`, `CharSequence`, `Array[Byte]`, `Chunk`, `ByteBuffer`, `Reader`
**Output formats:** `String`, `Array[Byte]`, `Chunk`, `ByteBuffer`, `Writer`
**Typed conversion:** `as[A]` / `asUnsafe[A]` via `JsonDecoder`

### Type Class Integration
- `JsonEncoder[A]` and `JsonDecoder[A]` type classes
- Explicit `JsonBinaryCodec` takes priority over schema derivation

### String Interpolators
- `p"path.to[0].field"` - Path construction
- `j"""{"key": "value"}"""` - JSON literal parsing
- Implemented for both Scala 2 (blackbox macros) and Scala 3 (inline/quotes)

### DynamicValue Interop
- `toDynamicValue` / `fromDynamicValue` with full round-trip fidelity
- `toKV` / `fromKV` for flat path-value representation
- Temporal values serialize to ISO format strings

---

## Testing

- **1306 tests pass** on JVM
- Compiles on **JVM, JS, Native**
- Compiles on **Scala 2.13 and Scala 3.3**
- Passes **scalafmt** validation

---

## Scope Notes

`JsonPatch` and `JsonSchema` are working but minimal placeholders, consistent with the issue marking them out-of-scope. Patch functionality supports `diff`/`patch` round-trips. Schema validation covers common cases but is not a full JSON Schema implementation.




## Demo Video






https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5b1f59f3-dd41-4854-884c-a2c3939ddac3
title: Add Json data type (Issue #679)

author: MichaelWalker-git
body:
## Summary

Resolves #685 - Implements a complete JSON patching system with efficient diff algorithms.

### Features

**Json ADT**
- Sealed trait with Null, Bool, Num, Str, Arr, Obj variants
- Type-safe construction via `Json.obj`, `Json.arr`, `Json.str`, etc.
- Safe accessors returning `Option[T]`
- Transformation operations: `dropNulls`, `sortKeys`, `normalize`, `merge`
- Traversals: `foldDown`, `foldUp`, `transformDown`, `transformUp`
- Full `DynamicValue` bidirectional conversion

**JsonPatch**
- Efficient LCS (Longest Common Subsequence) algorithm for string and array diffs
- Operations: `Set`, `NumberDelta`, `StringEdit`, `ArrayEdit`, `ObjectEdit`
- Path-based navigation via `JsonPath` with `/` syntax
- Composition via `++` operator
- `PatchMode` support (Strict, Lenient, Clobber)
- `DynamicPatch` interoperability

**Algebraic Laws**
- L1: Left Identity - `empty ++ p == p`
- L2: Right Identity - `p ++ empty == p`
- L3: Associativity - `(p1 ++ p2) ++ p3 == p1 ++ (p2 ++ p3)`
- L4: Roundtrip - `source.diff(target).apply(source) == Right(target)`
- L5: Identity Diff - `json.diff(json) == empty`
- L6: Composition - `diff(a,b) ++ diff(b,c)` semantically equals `diff(a,c)`
- L7: Lenient subsumes Strict

## Test plan

- [x] Property-based tests for all algebraic laws using ZIO Test generators
- [x] Unit tests for Json construction, access, and transformations
- [x] Unit tests for all JsonPatch operations (Set, NumberDelta, StringEdit, ArrayEdit, ObjectEdit)
- [x] Tests for diff algorithm correctness on strings, arrays, objects, nested structures
- [x] Tests for patch composition
- [x] Tests for JsonPath navigation
- [x] Tests for error handling with Strict/Lenient modes
- [x] Tests for DynamicValue interoperability
- [x] Edge case tests (empty structures, deep nesting, unicode, large arrays)
title: feat(json): Add JsonPatch for JSON diff and patch operations

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/14a4f8d1-727e-47a0-a2e0-5ea3ac90626d

### Checklist

author: Bhaviikkk
body:
## Summary

Add MessagePack binary codec support to zio-blocks, following the same architecture as existing binary codecs.

## Changes

### New Module: `schema-messagepack`

- **MessagePackBinaryCodec** - Abstract base class with `valueType` specialization for primitive register optimization
- **MessagePackFormat** - Complete `Deriver[MessagePackBinaryCodec]` implementation

### Features

- ✅ **30 Primitive Types** - All standard types including `java.time.*`, `Currency`, `UUID`, `BigInt`, `BigDecimal`
- ✅ **Forward-compatible record decoding** - Uses field name lookup with HashMap, skips unknown fields
- ✅ **Recursive record support** - ThreadLocal cache for handling cyclic data structures
- ✅ **Default values, optional fields, empty collections** - Proper handling of missing fields during decode
- ✅ **DynamicValue encode/decode** - Full support for all DynamicValue variants
- ✅ **Custom codec injection** - Via optics for per-field customization
- ✅ **Register optimization** - Uses `valueType` for primitive specialization

### Dependencies

- `msgpack-core 0.9.8` (JVM only)

### Test Coverage

- 84 comprehensive tests covering all types and edge cases
- All tests pass locally

## Checklist

- [x] Code compiles without warnings
- [x] Tests pass (`sbt testJVM`)
- [x] Scalafmt check passes (`sbt scalafmtCheckAll`)

/claim #682 
/closes #682
title: feat: Add MessagePack binary codec support

author: lml-layer-system
body:
/claim #679
## Summary
This PR implements **Bounty #679** requirements: string interpolators and completion of the `Json` API as specified in the official sketch.
Addresses the rejection feedback where string interpolators were identified as the critical blocker**.
## What's Implemented
### 1. String Interpolators (The Blocker)
**Path Interpolator (`p"..."`)**
- Scala 2.13 + Scala 3 implementations
- Compile-time parsing and validation
- Examples: `p"users[0].name"`, `p"items[*]"`, ``p"`escaped.field`"``
**JSON Literal Interpolator (`json"..."`)**
- Scala 2.13 + Scala 3 implementations
- Type-safe interpolation using `JsonEncoder`
- Example: `json"""{"name": $userName, "age": $userAge}"""`
### 2. Complete Json API
Added 17 missing methods from the official sketch:
- `modifyOrFail`, `setOrFail`, `deleteOrFail`
- `insert`, `insertOrFail`
- `transformKeys`
- `project`, `partition`, `normalize`
- `foldDown`, `foldUp`, `foldDownOrFail`, `foldUpOrFail`
- `query`, `toKV`
- `check`, `conforms` (stubs)
- `patch`, `patchUnsafe`, `diff` (stubs)
### 3. Out-of-Scope Placeholders
Per the spec note: *"JsonPatch and JsonSchema are OUT-OF-SCOPE"*
Implemented as minimal stubs only:
- `JsonPatch`: sealed trait (10 lines)
- `JsonSchema`: sealed trait (4 lines)
- NO RFC 6902 implementation
- NO JSON Schema validation
## Files Changed
**9 files (7 new, 2 modified)**
**New:**
- `PathMacros.scala` (Scala 2 + 3)
- `JsonMacros.scala` (Scala 2 + 3)
- `package.scala` / `interpolators.scala` (user APIs)
- `JsonPlaceholders.scala` (out-of-scope stubs)
**Modified:**
- `Json.scala` - Added 17 methods
- `JsonSelection.scala` - Minor support
## Compilation Proof
```bash
sbt schemaJVM/compile

author: bikramadhikari001
body:
## Summary

This PR implements a complete Json ADT for ZIO Blocks as specified in issue #679.

/claim #679

## Implementation Overview

This implementation provides a fully-featured Json data type with:
- Complete ADT with 6 cases (Object, Array, String, Number, Boolean, Null)
- Type-safe navigation and manipulation
- DynamicValue interop
- Schema-based encoding/decoding
- Comprehensive error handling
- 57 passing tests

## Features Implemented

### Core ADT
- Complete type hierarchy (Object, Array, String, Number, Boolean, Null)
- Type testing methods (isObject, isArray, isString, isNumber, isBoolean, isNull)
- Type filtering methods (asObject, asArray, asString, asNumber, asBoolean, asNull)
- Value accessors (fields, elements, stringValue, numberValue, booleanValue)

### Navigation
- Path-based navigation with DynamicOptic
- Key-based navigation for objects
- Index-based navigation for arrays
- JsonSelection fluent API with error accumulation

### Manipulation
- modify(path, f) - Transform values at path
- modifyOrFail(path, pf) - Partial function modification with error handling
- set(path, value) - Set value at path
- setOrFail(path, value) - Set with error handling
- delete(path) - Delete values at path
- deleteOrFail(path) - Delete with error handling
- merge(other) - Recursive object merging

### Transformation
- transformUp(f) - Bottom-up transformation
- transformDown(f) - Top-down transformation
- transformKeys(f) - Transform object keys
- filter(p) - Keep matching values
- filterNot(p) - Remove matching values

### Normalization
- sortKeys - Sort object keys alphabetically
- dropNulls - Remove null values
- dropEmpty - Remove empty objects/arrays
- normalize - Full normalization (sort keys + normalize numbers)

### Folding
- foldDown[B](z)(f) - Top-down fold with accumulator
- foldUp[B](z)(f) - Bottom-up fold with accumulator

### Querying
- query(p) - Select all values matching predicate

### KV Representation
- toKV - Flatten to path-value pairs
- fromKV - Assemble from path-value pairs

### DynamicValue Interop
- toDynamicValue - Convert Json to DynamicValue
- fromDynamicValue - Convert DynamicValue to Json

### Parsing & Encoding
- parse(input: String) - Parse JSON string to Json ADT
- encode(json: Json, config: WriterConfig) - Encode Json ADT to string

### Typed Encoding/Decoding
- JsonEncoder[A] type class with implicit priority
- JsonDecoder[A] type class with implicit priority
- from[A](value: A) - Encode typed value to Json
- as[A] - Decode Json to typed value
- Automatic schema derivation via Schema.derived

### Error Handling
- JsonError with path tracking (DynamicOptic)
- Conversion from SchemaError
- Optional offset/line/column information

### Smart Constructors
- number(Int), number(Long), number(Double), number(BigDecimal), number(String)

## Files Added/Modified

- **Json.scala** (1,491 lines) - Core Json ADT implementation
- **JsonError.scala** (63 lines) - Error type with path tracking
- **JsonSelection.scala** (174 lines) - Fluent selection API
- **JsonEncoder.scala** (81 lines) - Type class for encoding
- **JsonDecoder.scala** (87 lines) - Type class for decoding
- **JsonSpec.scala** (523 lines) - Comprehensive test suite

**Total: ~2,419 lines of production code**

## Test Coverage

**57 tests passing (100%)**

- Json ADT creation (6 tests)
- Navigation (6 tests)
- Type filtering (2 tests)
- DynamicValue conversion (7 tests)
- Comparison (2 tests)
- Merge (2 tests)
- Transformation (4 tests)
- Manipulation (5 tests)
- Parsing and Encoding (5 tests)
- JsonEncoder/JsonDecoder (5 tests)
- Normalization (2 tests)
- Folding (3 tests)
- Query (2 tests)
- KV representation (6 tests)

## Code Quality

- Idiomatic Scala patterns (pattern matching, for-comprehensions)
- Performance-focused (while loops for iteration, lazy vals)
- Cross-platform compatible (Scala 2.13 & 3)
- Comprehensive scaladoc with examples
- Follows ZIO Blocks conventions (Vector, not List)
- Zero placeholders - fully implemented
- All tests passing
- Compiles successfully

## Design Decisions

### Implicit Priority Resolution
JsonEncoder and JsonDecoder use a two-tier implicit priority system:
- **High priority**: Explicit JsonBinaryCodec instances
- **Low priority**: Schema-derived codecs via Schema.derived

This allows users to provide custom codecs while falling back to automatic derivation.

### JsonSelection API
Provides a fluent API for navigating and filtering JSON with automatic error accumulation, similar to ZIO Query.

### DynamicOptic Integration
Uses DynamicOptic for path-based navigation, ensuring consistency with the rest of ZIO Blocks.

### Number Representation
Numbers are stored as strings to preserve precision and avoid floating-point issues, following JSON best practices.

## Commit History

1. feat(json): add core Json ADT with type hierarchy and navigation
2. feat(json): add DynamicValue conversion and manipulation methods
3. feat(json): add transformation and filtering methods
4. test(json): add comprehensive test suite for Json ADT
5. feat(json): implement path navigation and transformation methods
6. feat(json): implement manipulation methods (modify, set, delete)
7. feat(json): add parsing and encoding with JsonBinaryCodec
8. feat(json): implement JsonEncoder/JsonDecoder type classes
9. chore: remove implementation plan
10. feat(json): add optional methods (normalize, fold, query, toKV/fromKV)

## Testing

All tests pass:
```bash
sbt "project schemaJVM" "testOnly *JsonSpec"
# 57 tests passed. 0 tests failed. 0 tests ignored.
```

## Related Issues

Closes #679

## Checklist

- [x] Implementation is complete
- [x] All tests pass
- [x] Code compiles successfully
- [x] Follows project conventions
- [x] Comprehensive documentation
- [x] No placeholders or TODOs
- [x] Professional commit history

author: orbin123
body:
This PR adds a compile-time string interpolator `p"..."` that parses path selector expressions and produces `DynamicOptic` instances with **zero runtime overhead**.

All parsing and validation happen at compile time. Invalid paths fail fast with clear, position-aware error messages.



### What’s Included

#### Core Parser (Shared)

| File | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `PathParser.scala` | Recursive-descent parser with position tracking. Supports fields, array indices/ranges, map keys, and variant cases. Includes overflow checks and 10K range limit. |
| `ASTBuilder.scala` | Converts parsed segments into `DynamicOptic.Node` structures. Clean split between parsing and AST building. |
| `ErrorReporter.scala` | Generates precise compile-time errors with visual pointers and hints. |

#### Scala 2.13 Macro

| File | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `scala-2/PathMacros.scala` | Whitebox macro using `Context`. Extracts literal from `StringContext`, parses, and emits optic tree. |
| `scala-2/PathInterpolator.scala` | Implicit class enabling `p"..."` syntax with Scaladoc. |

#### Scala 3 Macro

| File | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `scala-3/PathMacros.scala` | Inline macro using `scala.quoted` and `valueOrAbort`. |
| `scala-3/PathInterpolator.scala` | Extension method on `inline StringContext` (required for compile-time extraction). |



### Supported Syntax

```scala
p".users"        // Field
p".items[0]"     // Index
p".items[0:5]"   // Range
p".items[*]"     // All elements
p"""{"key"}"""   // String map key
p"{42}"          // Int map key
p"{*}"           // All values
p"{*:}"          // All keys
p"<Success>"     // Variant case
```

### Safety Guarantees
	•	Max range size: 10,000 (prevents OOM at compile time)
	•	Integer overflow rejected
	•	Interpolation disallowed: p".users[$i]" fails at compile time
	•	Position-aware error messages with exact location

### Tests
55 tests, shared across Scala 2.13 and Scala 3.3
Covers:
	•	Fields, indices, ranges, wildcards
	•	Map keys (string, int, bool, char, negative)
	•	Variants
	•	Nested/complex paths
	•	Unicode field names
	•	Escape sequences
	•	Edge cases (empty, long names, boundaries)

/claim #755
title: Add `p"..."` Path Interpolator Macro

author: ascender1729
body:
## Summary

This PR adds MessagePack binary serialization support to ZIO Blocks, porting the functionality from zio-schema-msgpack to the new ZIO Schema 2 architecture.

### Changes

- **New module: `schema-messagepack`**
  - `MessagePackBinaryCodec[A]` - Abstract class extending `BinaryCodec[A]` for MessagePack encoding/decoding
  - `MessagePackFormat` - Binary format with `Deriver[MessagePackBinaryCodec]` for automatic codec derivation

- **Supported types:**
  - All primitive types (Unit, Boolean, Byte, Short, Int, Long, Float, Double, Char, String)
  - Numeric types (BigInt, BigDecimal)
  - Date/Time types (Instant, LocalDate, LocalDateTime, LocalTime, Duration, Period, etc.)
  - UUID, Currency
  - Records (case classes) - encoded as MessagePack maps
  - Variants (sealed traits) - encoded with `_type` discriminator field
  - Sequences (List, Vector, etc.) - encoded as MessagePack arrays
  - Maps - encoded as MessagePack maps
  - Wrapper types
  - DynamicValue

- **Dependencies:**
  - `org.msgpack:msgpack-core:0.9.8`

### Example Usage

```scala
import zio.blocks.schema._
import zio.blocks.schema.messagepack.MessagePackFormat

case class Person(name: String, age: Int)
implicit val schema: Schema[Person] = Schema.derived
val codec = schema.derive(MessagePackFormat.deriver)

val person = Person("Alice", 30)
val bytes = codec.encode(person)
val decoded = codec.decode(bytes) // Right(Person("Alice", 30))
```

## Test Plan

- [x] Round-trip tests for all primitive types
- [x] Round-trip tests for records (simple, nested, with optional fields)
- [x] Round-trip tests for variants (sealed traits)
- [x] Round-trip tests for sequences (List, Vector)
- [x] Round-trip tests for maps
- [ ] CI checks pass

## Related Issues

/claim #682

Closes #682

author: Akshatthakur22
body:
/claim #755

This PR introduces a compile-time p"..." string interpolator for constructing DynamicOptic values safely and efficiently.

### What this PR adds
- Pure Scala path parser with full grammar support
- Compile-time validation using Scala 2.13 and Scala 3 macros
- Rejects any runtime interpolation (e.g., p"${x}")
- Generates efficient DynamicOptic(Vector(Node...)) code at compile time
- Provides clear and precise compile-time error reporting

### Why this feature is useful
- Ensures all paths are validated during compilation
- Eliminates runtime parsing and builder overhead
- Produces clean, static DynamicOptic values with zero allocation cost
- Improves both safety and performance for any path-heavy code

### Example
p"user.address.street"

Expands to:
DynamicOptic(Vector(
  Node.Field("user"),
  Node.Field("address"),
  Node.Field("street")
))

### Demo
Video demo: https://youtu.be/sqSwf18CANg

### Notes
- All parsing and validation is done at compile time
- No runtime parsing
- Works across JVM, JS, and Native

author: Bhaviikkk
body:
Summary
This PR implements comprehensive MessagePack binary codec support for ZIO Schema 2, resolving issue #682. The implementation provides a production-ready, forward-compatible codec that handles schema evolution gracefully while maintaining full type safety.
Motivation
MessagePack is a widely-used binary serialization format that offers significant advantages over JSON for performance-critical applications. This implementation brings MessagePack support to ZIO Schema 2, enabling developers to serialize and deserialize complex Scala data structures with minimal overhead.
Implementation Details
Architecture
The implementation follows ZIO Schema's established codec patterns, specifically mirroring the approach used in schema-avro:

MessagePackCodec.scala: Main codec implementation with Deriver[BinaryCodec]
Forward-compatible record encoding: Records are encoded as MessagePack maps (not arrays) to support schema evolution
Defensive decoding: Unknown fields are gracefully skipped, missing optional/collection fields default properly
Thread-safe caching: Uses ThreadLocal for recursive type handling
Key Features:-

**1. Complete Type Support**

-> All 30+ primitive types (including java.time.*, BigInt, BigDecimal, UUID, Currency)
->Records (case classes) with field-level customization
-> Variants (sealed traits, enums) with discriminator support
->Collections (Seq, Set, Map, Vector, Array, etc.)
-> Wrappers with validation
-> Option[A] and Either[A, B]
 ->DynamicValue for schema-less data
 -> Recursive types (via lazy codec derivation)

**2. Forward-Compatible Record Decoding**
Records are encoded as MessagePack maps with field names as keys, providing:

Unknown field tolerance: New fields added to the schema don't break old decoders
Order independence: Field order in the binary payload doesn't matter
Missing field handling: Optional fields and collections default gracefully when absent

This design choice prioritizes schema evolution over byte-size optimization, making it suitable for systems where data schemas evolve over time.

**3. Custom Codec Injection**
Supports injecting custom codecs at two levels:

By field optic: Override codec for specific record fields
By type name: Override codec for all instances of a type

**4. Error Handling**

Path-tracked errors for precise debugging (e.g., "record.address.street: expected string, got int")
Defensive bounds checking for collection sizes
Clear error messages with context

Testing
84 comprehensive tests covering:

 All primitive types (including edge cases: NaN, Infinity, Long.MinValue)
 Forward compatibility: out-of-order fields, unknown fields, missing optional fields
 Collections: empty, single-element, large (10k+ items)
 Records: simple, nested, with defaults, with custom codecs
 Variants: sealed traits, enums, discriminated unions
 Recursive types: linked lists, trees
 Unicode handling: emoji, multi-byte characters
 DynamicValue: round-trip preservation
 
**Related Issues**
Closes #682

**Acknowledgments**
This implementation draws inspiration from:

ZIO Schema's AvroCodec for architectural patterns
title: feat: Add MessagePack codec support for ZIO Schema 2 with forward-compatible record decoding

author: orbin123
body:
## Add Apache Thrift Binary Codec Support to ZIO Schema 2

This PR adds Apache Thrift binary wire format support to **ZIO Schema 2 (zio-blocks)** by introducing a new module called `schema-thrift`.

It enables automatic serialization and deserialization of any type `A` with a `Schema[A]` to and from Thrift binary format using Apache Thrift’s `TBinaryProtocol`.


### File Changes
```
zio-blocks/
├── schema-thrift/
│   ├── README.md
│   └── src/
│       ├── main/
│       │   └── scala/zio/blocks/schema/thrift/
│       │       ├── ChunkTransport.scala
│       │       ├── ThriftBinaryCodec.scala
│       │       └── ThriftFormat.scala
│       └── test/
│           └── scala/zio/blocks/schema/thrift/
│               └── ThriftCodecSpec.scala
└── build.sbt
```

### Features

- Automatic Thrift binary encoding and decoding for any `Schema[A]`
- Uses Apache Thrift `TBinaryProtocol`
- No manual codec writing required
- Fully compatible with existing ZIO Schema models


### Tests

Includes a comprehensive test suite covering:

- Primitive and time types  
- Records (including high-arity records)  
- Variants and enums  
- Collections  
- Recursive structures  

#### Wire Compatibility Cases

- Out-of-order fields  
- Sparse field IDs  
- Unknown field skipping for:
  - Strings  
  - Structs  
  - Lists  
  - Maps  

/claim #681
Fixes #681
title: Port Apache Thrift binary format to ZIO Schema 2 (schema-thrift) #681

author: khoinp1012
body:
## Summary
This PR optimizes `Chunk.byteChecksum` using a scalar unrolled loop, achieving a **~1.5x speedup** on the JVM.
## Changes
- **Optimized Checksum**: Replaced the functional fold/traversal in `byteChecksum` with a manual unrolled while-loop.
- **Platform Split**: Refactored `ChunkPlatformSpecific` into `jvm`, `js`, and `native` source roots. This allows JVM-specific optimizations (like this loop) without breaking other platforms.
- **Benchmarks**: Added `ScalarSpecializedBenchmark` to verify performance gains.
## Benchmarks
Results from `ScalarSpecializedBenchmark` (JVM 21):
| Size | Baseline (ops/s) | Optimized (ops/s) | Speedup |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1,000 | ~2,859,000 | ~4,172,000 | **1.46x** |
| 10,000 | ~276,000 | ~436,000 | **1.58x** |
Verified via `sbt "benchmarks/Jmh/run .*ScalarSpecializedBenchmark.*"`.
title: feat(chunk): scalar optimizations for checksum mechanism

author: lml-layer-system
body:
/claim #679

This PR implements the full JSON ADT for ZIO Blocks as defined in Bounty #679.

### Core Features Implemented
- Added `Json` sealed trait with 6 cases (`Object`, `Array`, `String`, `Number`, `Boolean`, `Null`)
- Implemented `JsonError` with DynamicOptic path tracking and detailed error accumulation
- Implemented `JsonSelection` API for fluent, multi-value navigation with accumulated errors
- Added `MergeStrategy` sealed hierarchy (Auto, Deep, Shallow, Replace, Concat, Custom)
- Implemented complete DynamicOptic path navigation (get / modify / set / delete)
- Added transformation operations: `transformUp`, `transformDown`, `filter`, `filterNot`
- Implemented normalization operations: `sortKeys`, `dropNulls`, `dropEmpty`
- Added `JsonEncoder[A]` and `JsonDecoder[A]` type classes with priority resolution
- Integrated parser support via `Json.parse(String | Array[Byte])`
- Added encoding methods: `json.encode` and `json.encodeToBytes`
- Implemented bidirectional `DynamicValue` interop

### Code Summary
Total implementation: ~1250 LOC across 4 Scala files  
All components manually verified and syntactically validated.

Closes #679
title: feat: implement complete Json ADT for ZIO Blocks (Bounty #679)

author: YadavAkhileshh
body:
This PR implements JsonPatch for issue #685

## What it does

JsonPatch lets you compute the difference between two JSON values and apply that diff to transform one into the other. It follows the same patterns as DynamicPatch but is simpler since JSON only has 4 primitive types and string-only object keys.

## Files added

- Basic JSON type with Null, Bool, Num, Str, Arr, Obj
- Error types for patch failures
- Three patch modes: Strict, Lenient, Clobber
- Main implementation with diff and apply methods
- LCS algorithm for efficient string and array diffs
- Property tests for all algebraic laws

## How it works

val source = Json.Obj(Vector("name" -> Json.Str("Alice")))
val target = Json.Obj(Vector("name" -> Json.Str("Bob")))

val patch = JsonPatch.diff(source, target)
patch(source, JsonPatchMode.Strict) // Right(target)

## Laws verified

All seven algebraic laws from the issue are tested:
- Monoid laws (identity and associativity)
- Roundtrip: diff(a, b) applied to a gives b
- Identity: diff(x, x) is empty
- Composition: patches can be combined
- Mode subsumption: Strict success means Lenient success

Closes #685
title: Add JsonPatch type for diffing and patching JSON values (#685)

author: Eliene-byte
body:
**Summary**
This PR implements the core of the structural migration system as requested in issue #679. It introduces the necessary data structures to handle data evolution within zio-blocks using DynamicValue and Schema.

**Changes**
Core Migration Logic: Introduced Migration[A, B] and DynamicMigration to handle transformations between different schema versions.

Migration Actions: Added MigrationAction ADT supporting operations such as AddField, DropField, Rename, and TransformValue.

JSON Support: Implemented a proper Json Algebraic Data Type (ADT) in the schema-json module, including Ordering support for structural comparisons.

Schema Integration: Leveraged toDynamicValue and fromDynamicValue from the core Schema class to ensure type-safe migrations through dynamic representations.

**Impact**
Allows developers to define reversible migrations between different versions of a data model.

Provides a standard JSON representation that can be used for structural diffing and patching.

**Checklist**
[x] Code compiles (verified via GitHub Actions).

[x] Migration actions are reversible.

[x] JSON ADT follows the project's structural **patterns.******
title: feat: implement structural migration system and Json type (#679)

author: jacanidjar
body: /claim #683 ## Feature: Full BSON Support for ZIO Schema 2 (Fixes #683) This PR implements the `schema-bson` module providing robust BSON serialization for ZIO Schema. ### Key Features - **Generic Derivation**: Support for primitives, case classes (Records), ADTs/Enums (Variants), Sequences, and Maps. - **Recursion Safe**: Implemented `LazyBsonCodec` to handle recursive types without `StackOverflow`. - **Annotation Support**: Responds to `@name` annotations for field renaming. - **DynamicValue Handling**: Implemented a custom "Envelope" codec (`{ "type": "...", "value": ... }`) to support generic `DynamicValue` round-trips and bypass BSON root-array limitations. ### Verification - Added comprehensive test suite: `BsonFormatSpec`. - **Status**: 25/25 tests PASSED. - Validated complex recursive structures and dynamic generic types. https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5e85f307-13e2-41e8-bd38-13c07e7da1fd
title: Feature: Full BSON Support for ZIO Schema 2 (Fixes #683)

author: khoinp1012
body:
This PR optimizes `Chunk.map` and `Chunk.flatMap` operations in `zio-blocks`. The primary focus was reducing boxing overhead for primitive types and improving iteration efficiency.

## Changes Made

### 1. Optimized `Chunk.map`
Rewrote the core mapping logic to avoid boxing when applying functions to primitive arrays.
- Specialized `mapChunk` implementations for `IntArray`, `ByteArray`, etc.
- Direct array allocation using the correct `ClassTag` from the first mapped value.

### 2. Stream Fusion in `Chunk.flatMap`
Improved the `flatMap` implementation to reduce intermediate allocations and improve cache locality during concatenation.

### 3. SIMD/Scalar Glue Implementation
Implemented missing companion object methods in `ChunkPlatformSpecific.scala` to support advanced `ByteArray` operations:
- **JVM**: Delegated to `SIMDUtils.java` using the Java Vector API (OpenJDK 16+).
- **JS/Native**: Provided robust scalar fallbacks for platform compatibility.

### 4. Build Configuration
Updated `build.sbt` to enable `jdk.incubator.vector` module support with necessary internal exports.

## Benchmark Results

Benchmarks were performed on 1,000 and 10,000 elements. The speedup factors represent the improvement over the scalar/unoptimized baseline in the current environment.

| Operation | Scalar/Baseline (ops/s) | Optimized/SIMD (ops/s) | Speedup |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `mapIntChunk` (1k) | ~110,000 | **237,426** | **2.16x** |
| `mapStringChunk` (1k) | ~28,000 | **51,276** | **1.83x** |
| `flatMapChunk` (100) | ~155,000 | **184,946** | **1.20x** |
| `byteChecksum` (1k) | 2,604,095 | **7,006,962** | **2.69x** |
| `toUpperCase` (1k) | 1,857,468 | **4,397,010** | **2.36x** |

## Verification Results

### Automated Tests
- `sbt chunkJVM/test`: **Passed** (246 tests)
- `sbt chunkJS/test`: **Passed** (211 tests)
- `SIMDCorrectnessSpec`: **Passed** (Verified `byteChecksum`, `indexOf`, `matchAny` on JVM and JS)

---

> [!NOTE]
> The performance gains are significant across the board. While the specific speedup multipliers (e.g., 2.4x) vary by hardware profile and operation type, the core goal of removing boxing and leveraging hardware acceleration has been successfully achieved.
title: Perf: Core Chunk Optimizations & SIMD Glue

author: khoinp1012
body:
# Walkthrough - PR 1: Core Chunk Optimizations

This PR optimizes `Chunk.map` and `Chunk.flatMap` operations in `zio-blocks`. The primary focus was reducing boxing overhead for primitive types and improving iteration efficiency.

## Changes Made

### 1. Optimized `Chunk.map`
Rewrote the core mapping logic to avoid boxing when applying functions to primitive arrays.
- Specialized `mapChunk` implementations for `IntArray`, `ByteArray`, etc.
- Direct array allocation using the correct `ClassTag` from the first mapped value.

### 2. Stream Fusion in `Chunk.flatMap`
Improved the `flatMap` implementation to reduce intermediate allocations and improve cache locality during concatenation.

### 3. SIMD/Scalar Glue Implementation
Implemented missing companion object methods in `ChunkPlatformSpecific.scala` to support advanced `ByteArray` operations:
- **JVM**: Delegated to `SIMDUtils.java` using the Java Vector API (OpenJDK 16+).
- **JS/Native**: Provided robust scalar fallbacks for platform compatibility.

### 4. Build Configuration
Updated `build.sbt` to enable `jdk.incubator.vector` module support with necessary internal exports.

## Benchmark Results

Benchmarks were performed on 1,000 and 10,000 elements. The speedup factors represent the improvement over the scalar/unoptimized baseline in the current environment.

| Operation | Scalar/Baseline (ops/s) | Optimized/SIMD (ops/s) | Speedup |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `mapIntChunk` (1k) | ~110,000 | **237,426** | **2.16x** 🚀 |
| `mapStringChunk` (1k) | ~28,000 | **51,276** | **1.83x** 🚀 |
| `flatMapChunk` (100) | ~155,000 | **184,946** | **1.20x** 📈 |
| `byteChecksum` (1k) | 2,604,095 | **7,006,962** | **2.69x** ⚡ |
| `toUpperCase` (1k) | 1,857,468 | **4,397,010** | **2.36x** ⚡ |

## Verification Results

### Automated Tests
- `sbt chunkJVM/test`: **Passed** (246 tests)
- `sbt chunkJS/test`: **Passed** (211 tests)
- `SIMDCorrectnessSpec`: **Passed** (Verified `byteChecksum`, `indexOf`, `matchAny` on JVM and JS)

---

> [!NOTE]
> The performance gains are significant across the board. While the specific speedup multipliers (e.g., 2.4x) vary by hardware profile and operation type, the core goal of removing boxing and leveraging hardware acceleration has been successfully achieved.
title: Perf: Core Chunk Optimizations & SIMD Glue

author: natinew77-creator
body:
/claim #471

## Unified TypeId System

This PR introduces a structure-aware type identity system for zio-blocks. The new TypeId replaces the legacy string-based TypeName, which could not distinguish between types with the same name but different type parameters.

### The Problem

The previous TypeName approach used string-based comparisons like `"scala.Option"`. This caused ambiguity when comparing `Option[Int]` vs `Option[String]` since both resolved to the same string. Schema derivation, caching, and codec lookup all relied on this flawed identity, leading to subtle bugs in complex type hierarchies.

### The Solution

TypeId captures the full structural identity of types including:
- The qualified owner path
- Type parameters with their arguments
- Variance information for subtyping checks
- Parent types for class hierarchy traversal

This enables correct behavior for applied types, type aliases, opaque types, enums, union types, intersection types, and recursive type structures.

### Cross-Platform Consistency

Separate macro implementations for Scala 2.13 and Scala 3.3 ensure identical runtime behavior across Scala versions. Both implementations produce structurally equivalent TypeId instances, verified through shared test suites.

### Test Coverage

**Total: 7,584+ tests passing across all platforms**

| Platform | Scala 3.3.7 | Scala 2.13.18 |
|----------|-------------|---------------|
| JVM | 1,951 | 1,873 |
| JS | 1,923 | 1,837 |
| Native | 1501 | 1501 |

**TypeId-Specific Tests:**
| Test Suite | Tests |
|------------|-------|
| TypeIdSchemaSpec (JSON round-trip) | 13 |
| TypeIdSerializationSpec | 8 |
| TypeId equality/hashCode | 66+ |

**Test Categories:**
- Primitives, records, variants, collections, maps
- Wrappers, sequences, dynamic values
- JSON codecs, TOON codecs, Avro codecs, BSON codecs
- Type conversions, round-trip reversibility
- Validation, schema evolution, edge cases
- Structural types, cross-compilation stability
- TypeId serialization and equality semantics

### TypeId Schema Instances (New)

Added `TypeIdSchemas.scala` providing Schema instances for TypeId infrastructure types:
- `Schema[TypeId[Any]]`, `Schema[TypeId[?]]`
- `Schema[TypeRepr]`, `Schema[TypeParam]`, `Schema[TypeBounds]`
- `Schema[Owner]`, `Schema[Owner.Segment]`
- `Schema[Variance]`, `Schema[Kind]`
- `Schema[TypeDefKind]`, `Schema[Constant]`, `Schema[Annotation]`

These enable JSON round-trip serialization of type metadata as requested.

## Changes Summary

- **typeid module**: Core TypeId data structures and algorithms
- **Scala 3 macros**: TypeIdMacros.scala for compile-time extraction
- **Scala 2 macros**: TypeIdMacros.scala with identical behavior
- **schema module**: TypeName → TypeId migration throughout
- **TypeIdSchemas**: Schema instances for all TypeId types

author: elskow
body:
/claim #682

### architecture tldr

- **old**: `MessagePackEncoder`/`MessagePackDecoder` classes that wrap `msgpack-core`.
- **new**: `MessagePackBinaryCodec[A]` with auto-deriver, thread-local buffer pool, etc. The code architecture itself mirrored how the `schema-toon` done

Added **`Either[A, B]` support** to the core schema library, which is required for MessagePack `Either` serialization (encoded as a single-entry map with `"left"` or `"right"` keys).

body:
## Summary

Implements `JsonPatch` type for computing diffs and applying patches to JSON values, as specified in issue #685. This implementation directly mirrors the existing `DynamicPatch` API, adapted for JSON's simpler data model.

## What's Included

| File | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `Json.scala` | JSON ADT with DynamicValue interop |
| `JsonPatch.scala` | Patch operations, diff/apply algorithms |
| `JsonError.scala` | Error handling |
| `JsonPatchLawsSpec.scala` | Property-based law tests |
| `JsonPatchSpec.scala` | Unit tests |

## Design

Mirrors `DynamicPatch` with JSON-specific simplifications:
- `NumberDelta(BigDecimal)` — single numeric delta type
- `StringEdit` — LCS-based string diffing  
- `ArrayEdit` — LCS-based array diffing
- `ObjectEdit` — field-based object diffing
- `JsonPatchMode` — Strict/Lenient/Clobber

## Verified Laws

All pass property-based tests:
- L1–L3: Monoid laws
- L4: Roundtrip `diff(a,b)(a) == Right(b)`
- L5: Identity `diff(j,j).isEmpty`
- L6: Composition
- L7: Lenient subsumes Strict

## Test Results

✅ 20 property-based tests (JsonPatchLawsSpec)
✅ 56+ unit tests (JsonPatchSpec)

---

/claim #685
